segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a black screen, as a deep-voiced male narrator introduces the 'Madrid's Galacticos era' as a controversial topic, questioning its success in transforming a club into a global brand versus its failure in building a balanced team, and whether business ambitions overshadowed footballing goals. As the narration unfolds, the screen transitions into a nostalgic, cinematic montage of filmstrip-style frames. These frames showcase various individuals in formal attire, including a man with glasses and a tie, alongside iconic football moments such as a Real Madrid player in a white 'SIEMENS Mobile' jersey holding up a 'FIGO 10' jersey, and a group of Real Madrid players posing together. The montage also features players in training or casual settings, including a bald man in a grey top, interspersed with close-ups of smiling faces and dynamic action shots, all framed by a vintage filmstrip aesthetic. As the narrator concludes by stating this is the story of the Galacticos era's success and failure, a dramatic, suspenseful orchestral score begins to build, accompanying the continuing montage. The sequence culminates in a stylized animation forming a circular 'balón' logo with a currency symbol, followed by a clear date display of 'May 20, 1998'. The segment ends abruptly with the distinct sound of a football being kicked, and a male voice clearly stating 'Roberto Carlos', followed by another voice saying 'no tire'.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a vintage filmstrip effect, displaying the date 'May 20, 1998' over a grainy football match, accompanied by the excited Spanish commentary of a goal being scored by Mijatovic, with crowd cheers and a whistle. A Real Madrid player in a white jersey lifts the Champions League trophy in jubilation, surrounded by teammates, as the commentary continues, celebrating a third goal by Raúl, making it an "impossible 3-0" for Valencia. The scene transitions to a close-up of Real Madrid president Lorenzo Sanz, a man in formal attire with glasses and a tie, first smiling while holding the trophy alongside a player, then later seen confidently smoking a cigar with the Real Madrid crest in the background. A calm, authoritative narrator explains, over a subtle, dramatic orchestral background music, that after winning the Champions League twice in three years, President Lorenzo Sanz entered the 2000 presidential elections with confidence. However, the filmstrip animation continues as on-screen text reveals the club's 'Total Debt €278M', directly aligning with the narrator's revelation of "underlying problems" and "debt problems" amounting to "278 million euros". The visual then shifts to a wide shot of the empty Santiago Bernabéu stadium, with 'REAL MADRID C.F.' spelled out in the stands, as the narrator further details the club's struggle to attract fans in the late 90s, stating the Santiago Bernabéu was "almost never filled". The final frame displays 'Average Attendance 99/00: 34241 Fans per match', reinforcing the narrator's point about declining fan engagement, all framed within the consistent nostalgic filmstrip animation.

segments.integrated_caption: A dramatic, slightly melancholic instrumental track plays as the video opens with a wide aerial shot of the dimly lit, empty Santiago Bernabéu stadium, displaying on-screen text 'Average Attendance 99/00' with a rising fan count from 35,218 to 36,316, accompanied by a graphic of five stadium seats. A narrator explains that despite numerous discounts and season ticket incentives, one of Spain's wealthiest men, Florentino Pérez, saw an opening in the elections, having lost in 1995 against Ramón Mendoza. A black-and-white portrait of Florentino Pérez, in formal attire with glasses and a patterned tie, is shown with his name banner, followed by a close-up of him smiling confidently, labeled '1995 Elections'. A historical photograph then shows him shaking hands with an older man amidst a crowd, including a woman with a notepad and a man with a microphone. The narrator details Pérez's clear goals: saving the club from accumulated debts, visually represented by a stark white background with a black square displaying 'Total Debt €278M', which could damage Madrid's ability to compete. Three Champions League trophies appear beside Pérez, symbolizing his ambition. Pérez is then seen by a large window, touching the glass and gazing contemplatively at a cityscape. The narrator reveals that Pérez's chances of winning were slim, leading him to make a historic promise to the socios. A newspaper clipping with the headline 'Florentino: “Si Gano, Figo vendrá al Madrid”' appears, followed by a close-up of Pérez smiling, with an overlay quoting his promise: 'If I win the presidency, I’ll bring Luis Figo from Barcelona. And if I fail in that, I’ll pay the cost of all season tickets for the socios.' The narrator confirms this was Florentino's pitch, and he won with over 55% of the votes. Pérez is then shown signing autographs for fans, including a young boy, in a lively, crowded setting. Grainy, blue-tinted archival footage shows him being greeted by men in suits, including a bald man in a grey top, as the narrator concludes that Figo was presented days later, marking the beginning of a new era for Real Madrid, signified by a close-up of a man in a white shirt and a final black screen with '00-01 Season'.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black-and-white archival photograph of a Real Madrid team, including players and officials, posing with a trophy, as a male narrator begins to explain that Perez wanted to replicate the glory of the 50s. This historical context is reinforced by a transition to a close-up of Santiago Bernabéu, identified by on-screen text, smiling beside another man, with the narrator stating Bernabéu, as ex-president, brought the best stars to Madrid. The Real Madrid crest then appears over a vintage photo of players, while the narrator lists historical names like Di Stefano. The narrative shifts to modern times with a portrait of Di Stefano, labeled 'Honorary President,' as the narrator confirms he was brought back in this role. A younger Luis Figo is then seen holding up a Real Madrid jersey with his name, symbolizing a pivotal transfer, with the narrator detailing how Figo became the first player to agree on selling 50% of his image rights, visually emphasized by a graphic showing the '50%' split. The focus then turns to Florentino Pérez, a man in formal attire with glasses, seen on the phone beside a 'GANA en MADRID' sign, as the narrator explains Florentino's primary goal was clearing the club's debts. A newspaper clipping reveals '46.200 millones de deuda' (46.2 million debt) and 'Guerra en el Madrid' (War in Madrid), visually illustrating the club's critical financial position, which the narrator confirms was quoted by Pérez in September 2000. The segment continues with a close-up of Fernando Redondo, with the narrator revealing his sale to Milan for 17 million euros was the first step to fix the financial problem. This decision sparked anger, visually depicted by black-and-white images of fans holding protest signs like 'REDONDO NO SE VENDE' and 'F. PEREZ PESETERO,' a reaction the narrator attributes to both fans and teammates. The final frame shows a quote from 'El Macao: Four Years with Real Madrid,' explaining how Redondo’s public support for Sanz created enemies with the new president, Pérez, underscoring the political and emotional tensions within the club, all set against a consistent, dramatic instrumental background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a Real Madrid player in a white 'Teka' jersey, mid-action, overlaid with a quote from 'El Macao: Four Years with Real Madrid.' A narrator explains this refers to Redondo's strong support for ex-president Lorenzo Sanz and his significant presence in the locker rooms. The scene transitions to a close-up of a bald man in a black shirt, identified by the narrator as Nicolas Anelka, with a graphic overlay indicating his €34.50m transfer to PSG after a single, troublesome season. This is followed by a newspaper clipping featuring Florentino Pérez with the headline 'Sobrevivir es un milagro' (Surviving is a miracle), as the narrator explains that previous transfers weren't enough to clear accumulated debt, and Pérez was reluctant to use the stock market option, indicating that financial operations would take time. The camera then pans across the grand, empty Santiago Bernabéu stadium. Next, a man in a suit and tie, identified on-screen and by the narrator as the new Sporting Director Jorge Valdano, appears in a close-up, followed by images of players Makélélé and Solari, whose transfers were made in the summer as part of many reinforcements. The video then cuts to Vicente del Bosque, labeled as Manager, with a serious expression, as the narrator states that under his leadership, Madrid started the season exceptionally well, with Figo being a main catalyst. The narrative shifts to grainy, blue-tinted archival footage of a Real Madrid match, showing a goal being scored and celebrated, followed by a crowd of fans in the stands. On-screen text identifies the match as '00/01 La Liga – GW6 BARCELONA – MADRID, OCT – 2000', while the narrator describes this as a tough, early test for Madrid at the fiery Camp Nou, following Figo's 'treason' as seen by the Culés. The final frames show a Real Madrid player in a white number 10 jersey celebrating with teammates, capturing the emotional high of a victory, as the narrator begins to describe the fans' welcome.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a Real Madrid player in a white number 10 jersey, captured mid-action and then celebrating a goal with teammates, accompanied by intense crowd noise and a commentator speaking of a "former star with passionate anger." A graphic then reveals the match result: '00/01 La Liga – GW6 BARCELONA 2-0 MADRID OCT – 2000'. The commentator explains that "Madrid lost the match by two goals and continued to lose two of the next three matches," sparking "endless criticism," visually reinforced by a stylized graphic featuring Real Madrid players R. Carlos and Casillas with the text 'El Madrid, a placer en peor'. However, the commentator quickly adds that "Los Merengues was back on track in no time, as they won an impressive eight consecutive matches in La Liga," as archival footage shows a goal being scored and celebrated by fans. The narrative continues with Madrid's resurgence, leading to their trip to Tokyo for the Intercontinental Cup, where they faced "a Boca Juniors side led by manager Carlos Bianchi." Visually, a man in formal attire with glasses and a patterned tie (Florentino Pérez) is seen at a formal event, followed by a bald man in a grey top (Carlos Bianchi) shouting intensely. A filmstrip animation then shows a Boca Juniors player celebrating a goal, confirming the match result: 'BOCA 2-1 MADRID 29 NOV, 2000'. The commentator details how "after Riquelme's impressive showing, along with goals of Palermo, Madrid lost the title," and "Perez was concerned with how Madrid's loss affected their ability to capture the Asian market." This is followed by a graphic displaying the 'FIFA CLUB OF THE CENTURY' trophy, showing Real Madrid leading at 42.35%, and a handshake between two men in suits, one of whom is the man with glasses and tie (Pérez). Despite the earlier loss, "Florentino came back to receive the award for Club of the Century from FIFA, along with Di Stéfano," who was a "major factor for winning that award." The segment concludes with match footage from '00/01 La Liga – GW20 VALENCIA 0-1 MADRID JAN – 2001', showing a Real Madrid player scoring against a diving goalkeeper, emphasizing their continued competitive efforts.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dynamic montage of Real Madrid’s 2000-01 season, highlighting a vital 1-0 win against Valencia in January 2001. This is followed by a graphic displaying the 2000 Ballon d’Or results, where Luis Figo was presented with the prestigious award, winning ahead of Zidane. A newspaper-style graphic then announces 'El Madrid se va' alongside a 2-2 draw against Barcelona in March 2001, with a league table showing Real Madrid leading and favored to obtain the La Liga title despite the draw. A tactical diagram appears, illustrating the attacking roles of top goal scorer Raúl Gonzalez and Guti, who was given attacking freedom on the pitch by manager Vicente del Bosque, making it Guti's best scoring season. Del Bosque, seen in a close-up with a stern, focused expression, observes intently. The diagram then shifts to show the rigid midfield duo of Makélélé and Helguera, followed by a moment of camaraderie between Helguera and a player in a red and yellow jersey. The narrative continues with Madrid facing Bayern Munich after a win against Galatasaray in the Champions League quarter-finals. A tense moment on the pitch shows Real Madrid players, including Figo, appearing dejected after a match, reflecting a touch of arrogance and overconfidence. A quote from Steve McManaman overlays the scene, where he states in his biography that the team prepared for European matches by watching only a single 15-minute video, suggesting a shift in mindset or strategy during this pivotal season for Real Madrid.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Steve McManaman in a white Real Madrid jersey, as a narrator discusses the team's minimal video analysis, setting a tone of overconfidence. This transitions to archival footage of the 2001 Champions League semifinal, where Bayern Munich scores against Real Madrid, with the narrator detailing Madrid's loss at home in the first leg due to a great performance by Oliver Kahn, whose save is highlighted by a commentator shouting, "Denied by Kahn!" The footage continues with a second Bayern goal, confirming Madrid's exit, followed by jubilant Bayern fans celebrating. A shift in narrative occurs with a newspaper clipping featuring Florentino Pérez and the headline 'FLORENTINO HOPES TO CLEAR DEBT', as the narrator explains how Madrid City Council approved Florentino's request to change the club's training ground from green land for sports to a constructible area for business, leading to a significant jump in value. This is visually represented by a graphic of 'CIUDAD DEPORTIVA, MADRID' transforming from 'GREEN LAND' to 'CONSTRUCTABLE AREA'. The mood then shifts to triumph, showing a Real Madrid player celebrating a goal during a 5-0 La Liga victory over Alavés, followed by players lifting the La Liga trophy amidst confetti, with the narrator confirming this as their first league title since 1997. Another newspaper clipping, 'Carta blanca', with Florentino Pérez superimposed, appears as the narrator reveals that the City Council approved the selling of the newly converted training ground, allowing Madrid to earn approximately 446 million. The segment concludes with a Google Earth satellite view of the 'OLD TRAINING GROUND' in Madrid, overlaid with the cost '€446.7 MILLION', emphasizing the financial scale of the club’s redevelopment plans.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a Google Earth satellite view of Madrid, highlighting the 'OLD TRAINING GROUND' with a black-and-white dashed outline and a cost label of '€446.7 MILLION'. A calm male narrator explains this deal generated "446.7 million euros," clearing club debt and enabling the construction of a new layout labeled 'CIUDAD REAL MADRID' for a "cheaper price" of '€100 MILLION', illustrating a major redevelopment project. The scene shifts to a golden-hued exterior shot of the Santiago Bernabéu stadium, its name prominently displayed, as the narrator details how this financial move allowed Perez to invest additional money into the team. A black screen then displays '01-02 Season', setting the timeline, followed by a close-up of Zinedine Zidane at the UEFA Awards Gala in Monaco, August 2000, dressed in a beige blazer, as the narrator reveals that acquiring Zidane's signature was Perez's first step. Gentle acoustic guitar music begins as the narrative moves to a black-and-white image of Florentino Pérez, smiling, beside a text box asking 'Do you want to play for Madrid?'. The narrator explains this interaction occurred at the Euro Gala in August 2000, with Perez passing the question on a napkin: "Would you like to play for Real Madrid?" Zidane’s affirmative 'YES' in bold letters appears, confirmed by the narrator stating Zidane's reply on the napkin was "Yes." The phrase 'MISIÓN: ZIDANE' then appears in large, dramatic typography over a newspaper backdrop, emphasizing the significance of the transfer. The narrator continues, explaining Florentino returned in summer 2001, leading to a graphic showing a failed negotiation between Valdano and Moggi, marked with a red 'X' and '€90M'. The narrator clarifies that "tough negotiations" forced Madrid to "break the transfer record to buy Zidane from Juventus." This is followed by Zidane holding a Real Madrid jersey with 'ZIDANE 5' printed on it, superimposed over a newspaper headline 'El hombre de los 12.500 millones' and a cost overlay of '€77.5M', symbolizing the historic transfer. The final frame shows a press conference with Florentino Pérez, Zidane, and other officials, including a man in a grey suit, standing before a backdrop with 'CANAL+' logos, marking the official announcement of the signing.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Florentino Pérez, in a suit, standing beside Zinedine Zidane, who holds up a white Real Madrid jersey with 'ZIDANE 5', symbolizing his official unveiling. A narrator explains the club's ambition to be "the best in the world" and attract "global fans," aiming to surpass Manchester United in commercial revenue. The scene transitions to a young boy excitedly holding a similar jersey, followed by a graphic overlay showing Zidane giving a thumbs-up, with text indicating 'Image Rights 20%' and 'Salary €5M/YR After Tax', while the narrator details Zidane's agreement to exchange "90% of his image rights to Madrid" for "nearly 5 million euros a year after taxes." Pérez is then seen signing autographs for fans in a crowded setting, as the narrator explains that "Pérez and Madrid planned for a special year" to celebrate their "100-year anniversary." This is followed by animated graphics of the Real Madrid crest evolving from 1902 to 2002, accompanied by a quote from Carlos Pinedo stating, 'Winning the club's first treble in our hundred-year history is our grand objective,' reinforcing the club's ambitious centenary goal. The video then cuts to grainy black-and-white archival footage of a 1960 match at Hampden Park, with the narrator mentioning that the Champions League final will be held there, where "Alfredo Di Stefano and co. won their fifth European Cup." This is followed by a graphic showing Luis Figo with his quote: 'We believe winning in Glasgow is sort of our destiny,' as the narrator confirms Figo's belief that "La Novena will be their destiny." The segment concludes with a close-up of Vicente del Bosque, a man with a mustache, looking thoughtful with a black bar obscuring the lower frame, while the narrator poses the strategic question: "But Del Bosque had a problem: Where will Zidane fit in the lineup?"

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black-and-white montage of a distressed man, symbolizing a crisis, followed by the bold text 'Problema Zidane'. An English narrator questions "where are the defensive transfers?" and notes that "the media had the same question with regards to the Frenchman," as Zidane played at Juventus and for France in a position "that is non-existent at Madrid." A split-screen shows Zidane in French and Juventus jerseys with tactical diagrams, illustrating his dual identity and influence. The narrator continues, explaining that "after a poor start to the season, as Madrid were a single point away from the relegation spots after six matches," a league table highlights Real Madrid’s poor form with only 5 points from 15 games, emphasized by the text 'Madrid se tambalea' (Madrid is wobbling). A graphic of Zidane holding a Real Madrid jersey with 'ZIDANE 5' appears, reinforcing the transfer narrative. "A lot of criticism was targeting the Frenchman," especially after Madrid won their first four Champions League matches "without Zidane" due to suspension, as the Champions League form graphic shows Salgado with four consecutive wins. The narrator explains Zidane's suspension was "after being sent off for Juve last season." Uplifting background music begins as the scene cuts to live-action footage of Zidane in a Juventus jersey, dribbling and scoring, with the narrator stating, "the Frenchman quickly proved the critics wrong and finally showed his value." Excited Spanish commentators shout "Zidane!" and then "Gol! Golazo de Zinedine Zidane!" celebrating a spectacular goal, followed by repeated exclamations of "lo que ha hecho Zidane!" (what Zidane has done!), emphasizing his impressive play. The background music continues as an English narrator states, "Madrid were back on track after Del Bosque found the right lineup." The video opens with a black-and-white grid background displaying a Spanish quote, 'No veo fichajes, no hay stars' (I don’t see signings, there are no stars), attributed to Carlos Pinedo, who appears in a suit and tie with a serious expression. A narrator explains that new defenders were sought after players like Karanka and Campo, who are seen in a composite image flanking Pinedo, left the club. The scene transitions to highlight Makélélé in a white Real Madrid jersey, with a text overlay calling him the 'MOST IMPORTANT PLAYER ON THE FIELD.' The narrator elaborates that Makélélé, though regarded by Zidane as crucial, felt under-appreciated due to his €1.2M/year salary, especially when compared to Zidane's €5M/year, shown in a side-by-side comparison. This leads to the introduction of Florentino Pérez, a man in a suit and glasses against a grid background, with the phrase 'Zidanes Y Pavones' appearing above him. The narrator explains this policy as buying 'stars' (labeled 'CRACS' and featuring players like Zidane, Figo, and Ronaldo in a top row) who receive high salaries and generate commercial revenue, while balancing the squad with 'youth products' or 'cantera' (academy players, shown in a bottom row) who are paid very low salaries. The narrator critiques this approach, stating it creates a 'middle class of players' who don't receive the attention and respect they deserve. The segment concludes with a Real Madrid player in a white jersey, his eyes obscured by a black bar, overlaid with an EL PAÍS quote: 'La política de 'zidanes y pavones' elimina a los de clase media,' reinforcing the narrator's point and a quote from Solari about the policy potentially causing the 'extinction of the middle class.'

The financial stakes are emphasized with '€15M' appearing next to Pérez’s image, as the narrator reveals that Joan Gaspar came in, offering 15 million euros for Morientes, who was out of favor. The final frames introduce 'Opción Morientes' with a black-and-white image of Morientes in a Real Madrid training kit, juxtaposed with a newspaper article, suggesting him as an alternative signing. The sequence ends with a return to the split-screen, now with Morientes’s image replacing the financial figure, reinforcing the narrative of transfer strategy and missed opportunities, with the narrator questioning why Barcelona was helping Madrid, before concluding that Gaspar withdrew his offer an hour before the market closed.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dramatic newspaper headline 'Morientes NO' in bold white letters on a red background, signaling a failed transfer, as a narrator explains a previous offer aimed to leave Florentino Pérez without funds for the Ronaldo transfer. The scene transitions to a split-screen negotiation graphic featuring Florentino Pérez (top-left) and Massimo Moratti (top-right), with a man in a suit gesturing in the bottom-left, while the narrator details how Moratti buying Crespo saved Madrid €10 million due to an abundance of strikers. A financial arrow labeled '€36M' points from Moratti to a player image, which then changes to Ronaldo, as the narrator explains Ronaldo's price dropped, and Moratti agreed to sell the Brazilian for €35 million plus Solari, or €10 million in December. A central graphic shows Ronaldo holding a 'RONALDO' sign, flanked by Pérez and Moratti, illustrating a €35M transfer deal, which is then crossed out with a red 'X' over '€10M', as Solari refused to transfer and Pérez hasn't paid the €10 million Moratti wanted. The scene cuts to a newspaper headline 'TERREMOTO' (Earthquake) with Ronaldo in a Real Madrid jersey, followed by 'Llega lesionado' (Arrives injured), highlighting his arrival in poor condition, a fact confirmed by the narrator who states Ronaldo came injured. Archival footage then shows Ronaldo in action during a match, scoring two goals in a 5-2 victory over Alavés, with on-screen text confirming the date and score, accompanied by an excited Spanish commentator shouting "GOL! de Ronaldo!" twice amidst crowd cheers, as the narrator describes Ronaldo entering from the bench after missing three matches and scoring, with expectations "through the roof." The segment concludes with a newspaper headline 'Efecto Ronaldo' (Ronaldo Effect) and a graphic showing his goal statistics, emphasizing his immediate impact despite initial setbacks, though the narrator notes that in reality, Madrid struggled.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black-and-white newspaper graphic titled 'Ronaldo, negado como titular,' displaying Ronaldo’s recent match statistics showing 0 goals in 435 minutes across five games, as a narrator explains his struggle to score in the upcoming months. This transitions to a split-screen: on the left, a newspaper headline reads 'Pitos en el Bernabéu al equipo blanco y a Ronaldo' (Whistles in the Bernabéu aimed at the blancos & Ronaldo), while the right shows Ronaldo in a white Real Madrid jersey with 'RONALDO 11,' walking dejectedly, with the narrator confirming he was whistled by the Bernabéu after a goalless draw. The footage then cuts to a November 2002 match between Barcelona and Madrid, where Ronaldo, in a white 'SIEMENS mobile' jersey, appears frustrated walking off the field after a 0-0 draw, amidst loud crowd whistles and chants, with fans holding a banner referencing Luis Figo’s controversial move, as the narrator describes Figo's hostile return to Camp Nou and the match ending in a stalemate. The scene shifts to a 2002 Intercontinental Cup match where Ronaldo scores a goal against Olimpia, celebrating with teammates, including Figo ('FIGO 10'), as the narrator details Madrid's trip to Tokyo and their title win. Ronaldo is then seen holding the 2002 Ballon d'Or trophy, with a graphic listing his 161 votes, as the narrator highlights this award for his World Cup performances. The video then shows Ronaldo in training and scoring a goal in a match against a blue and white team, followed by a close-up of his 'RONALDO 11' jersey as he celebrates, with the narrator noting his return to his best in the league, losing only a single game in 22 despite persistent fitness issues. The segment concludes with a Champions League quarterfinals graphic, setting up Manchester United vs. Real Madrid, featuring Sir Alex Ferguson and Vicente del Bosque, as the narrator states Madrid faced United.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen graphic announcing the 'quarterfinals of the Champions League' between Manchester United and Real Madrid, featuring images of Sir Alex Ferguson and Vicente del Bosque, as a narrator discusses the match, which was 'filled with rumors about Beckham's potential summer transfer to Madrid.' This transitions to a newspaper-style graphic with 'Galáctico' and a photo of David Beckham clapping, setting the tone for Real Madrid’s star-studded era. Archival footage shows a goal being scored in a packed stadium, followed by on-screen text indicating the 2002-03 Champions League quarterfinals, with Real Madrid defeating Manchester United 3-1 in April 2003. The narrator explains that 'after winning by three goals to one at the Santiago Bernabeu, the two teams entered Old Trafford to book a place in the next round.' The footage then shifts to players entering the pitch, including a close-up of Ronaldo in a black Real Madrid jersey with 'RONALDO 11' on the back, as the narrator states that 'all the questions surrounding Ronaldo from the fans were evaporated after a historic performance.' Ronaldo proceeds to score three goals in quick succession, each met with roaring crowd sounds and excited commentary, with shouts like 'Ronaldo shot!', 'Heartbreaker!', 'it's Ronaldo!', and 'What a hat-trick!' as on-screen text marks the score progression: 0-1, 1-2, and 2-3. Ronaldo celebrates each goal with open arms and a raised hand. A final graphic shows the aggregate score of 5-6 in favor of Real Madrid. The segment concludes with a new graphic for the 'semifinals' of the Champions League, pitting Juventus against Real Madrid, with images of Marcello Lippi and Vicente del Bosque, as the narrator notes that 'Madrid faced another tougher test in the semifinals against Juventus,' a series 'filled with absences and injuries.'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a newspaper clipping headlined 'Raul accelerates his recovery to play in Turin,' as a narrator explains Raul missed the first leg of the Champions League semifinal, which Real Madrid won 2-1 at the Santiago Bernabeu. The scene transitions to live-action footage from the 2002-03 Champions League semifinals, 2nd leg in Turin, showing Real Madrid players in white jerseys, including Figo (number 10) and Ronaldo, celebrating a goal. The narrator details how 'Los Blancos' went to the Delle Alpi without Makelele due to suspension, conceded three goals, and Buffon saved Figo's penalty, a moment underscored by intense crowd cheering and a commentator shouting 'Buffon!' Juventus equalizes with Del Piero scoring in the 43rd minute, updating the aggregate score to Juventus 3-2 Madrid. Despite Ronaldo entering the match after an injury, 'Los Merengues' lost, and the final whistle blows on a 3-3 draw in Turin, eliminating Real Madrid on away goals. The aftermath is captured through newspaper headlines like 'Máxima tensión' and 'Referéndum en el Bernabéu,' reflecting the intense public and media criticism that piled up on Del Bosque and captain Hierro, who was exposed athletically. The video then cuts to a montage of Real Madrid’s struggles, including a Copa del Rey quarterfinal loss to Mallorca (4-1), where they crashed out losing by four goals, and a La Liga defeat to Sociedad (4-2), described as a pivotal loss against the underdogs. Ronaldo is visibly dejected, and the segment concludes with a split-screen graphic contrasting Ronaldo and Nihat, symbolizing the team’s internal conflict and the emotional toll of the season’s failures.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen graphic featuring Ronaldo in a Real Madrid jersey and Nihat in an Espanyol jersey, set against newspaper clippings and the bold text 'Va la Liga,' as a narrator describes the intense 'tug of war' between the two teams for the title. The scene transitions to a bustling press conference filled with journalists and cameras, capturing the media frenzy surrounding Real Madrid. A close-up of David Beckham’s face appears over a newspaper headline 'Locura Beckham,' as the narrator reveals Madrid's 'unwelcome and untimely news' of Beckham signing just days before a pivotal match, upsetting the manager and players. A man identified as Valdano, in a suit, is then seen speaking at a press conference, with a quote overlay stating, 'It was impossible to sit on that information for a week,' as the narrator explains Valdano's plea to the media to announce the transfer to quell rumors. The narrative shifts to archival footage of a La Liga match between Madrid and Atletico Bilbao on June 22, 2003, showing a Real Madrid goal, followed by celebrations including Hierro and Ronaldo, and the final score of 3-1, as the narrator confirms Madrid 'did their job' and lifted their 29th title. The narrator highlights Hierro's substitution minutes before the end, receiving a big ovation, marking his last steps as a Madrid player in the Bernabeu. The segment concludes with a reflective quote from Steve McManaman, displayed over a black background, describing the emotional difficulty of celebrating the title after being knocked out of the Champions League, with the narrator mentioning this detail from McManaman's biography.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black-and-white close-up of Steve McManaman, serious in a suit, as text from 'El Macao: Four Years with Real Madrid' scrolls, discussing player grievances over club policy. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a tense moment from the 2004 Copa del Rey Final between Madrid and Zaragoza, showing players in yellow and black jerseys in formation, as a narrator confirms Madrid "lost after extra time." A Spanish commentator exclaims "¡Y gol! Ha sorprendido Galletti," as a dramatic goal by Galletti in the 112th minute seals Zaragoza's 2-1 victory, accompanied by crowd cheers. The scene shifts to a Champions League Round of 16 match in March 2004, where Madrid narrowly defeats Bayern Munich 1-0, with the narrator setting the stage for Madrid to face Monaco in the quarter-finals. A newspaper headline '¡Y viene Morientes!' highlights the striker's arrival, followed by a bold headline 'BALSAMO PARA LA CRISIS' (Balm for the Crisis) over a celebrating player in a white jersey. The narrative transitions to the Champions League Quarter-Finals second leg in April 2004, where Madrid, having won the first leg 4-2, faces Monaco with "huge confidence," which "grew after Raúl scored in the first half," as the narrator explains. Monaco now needs three goals to advance. Live-action footage shows intense play and goals, with a Spanish commentator shouting "¡Y gol!" and "¡Golazo! ¡Juli acaba de marcar el tercero de tacón!" as Juli scores a backheel goal, leading to an intense celebration with players in red and white jerseys embracing amidst crowd roars. The narrator then states Madrid "fell on the hands of Juli and the loaned out Morientes." The final frames depict a newspaper headline 'CRISIS' over a dejected player, followed by a black-and-white image of a Real Madrid player being tackled, with the headline 'Broncazo del Bernabéu a un Madrid humillado por Osasuna,' indicating a humiliating 0-3 defeat and fan backlash, as the narrator concludes they "lost" upon returning to La Liga duties. Throughout, a dynamic background score underscores the rollercoaster of emotions, from triumph to crisis, defining the 2003-04 season for Real Madrid.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic black-and-white newspaper headline, 'Broncazo del Bernabéu a un Madrid humillado por Osasuna,' visually reinforcing the male narrator's explanation of Real Madrid's humiliating 3-0 loss against Osasuna. A pensive Jorge Valdano is then shown in close-up as the narration continues, detailing how 'Los Blancos' lost all their last five matches of the season, finishing in fourth position, a decline visually represented by dynamic league tables showing them slipping from 2nd to 4th place between GW34 and GW36. The audio specifies that among these losses, Real Madrid was defeated at the Santiago Bernabéu by Barcelona, a moment depicted by archival footage of a 2004 La Liga match (Madrid 1-2 Barcelona), showing a goal and a dejected Raúl walking off, symbolizing the end of an era. The narration states that Carlos Queiroz was sacked and Valdano resigned, which is visually confirmed by a newspaper headline 'Valdano deja el Real Madrid.' A press conference image shows Valdano and Florentino Pérez smiling together, as Pérez brought in Antonio Camacho and reinforced the defense with Samuel, a move highlighted by a headline 'Exhiben a Samuel sin haber firmado el contrato,' all aimed at generating positive news for the upcoming presidential elections. A black screen marks the '04-05 Season' as subtle, dramatic background music begins, and the audio confirms Florentino Pérez's landslide re-election, visually depicted by a graphic showing his 94% vote on July 11, 2004. Pérez vowed to reinforce the team again, concluding with the Real Madrid crest on a grid background, symbolizing institutional continuity and change.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a tense scene of Real Madrid players on the bench, visibly frustrated and disengaged, with one player slumping forward in disappointment, as a narrator explains this was the third league match where Galacticos like Beckham and Raul were benched. The atmosphere shifts to a series of newspaper clippings and text overlays, highlighting internal team struggles with quotes like 'Mi trabajo no se ve plasmado' and 'No le puedo sacar el máximo rendimiento al equipo,' which the narrator attributes to Antonio Camacho upon his resignation, stating his work wasn't being reflected on the field and he couldn't get the most out of the team. A close-up of the stern-faced coach with a mustache, identified as Antonio Camacho, appears, followed by a trio of players—David Beckham, Guti, and Zinedine Zidane—looking serious and focused. The narrator then recounts Florentino Perez's hints about facing big problems if stars aren't played, and the challenges faced by newly appointed manager Ramon who tried to play by unwritten rules. This narrative transitions to a split-screen showing a man in a suit (likely a club official or commentator) and a player in a white jersey, with text emphasizing 'punishment to whoever doesn’t comply,' indicating strict discipline. The scene cuts to a full team lineup of Real Madrid players in white 'SIEMENS mobile' jerseys, standing in formation on the pitch, before shifting to a tactical diagram from November 20, 2004, showing player positions with Beckham and Guti highlighted in red, which the narrator details as Madrid's lineup against Barcelona at the Camp Nou, noting that Beckham and Guti in a more defensive role was never the best solution. The video then starkly displays the match result: 'BARCELONA 3-0 MADRID,' a significant defeat that the narrator attributes to Ronaldinho and Eto's performances. A newspaper headline 'SACCHI, THE SHIELD OF FLORENTINO' appears, followed by a close-up of a bald man, identified by the narrator as Arrigo Sacchi, whom Perez brought in as a football director to fix the team's issues. The final frames show another match result: 'MADRID 0-1 SEVILLA,' reinforcing the team’s poor form during the 2004-05 season, all set against a slightly dramatic background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic newspaper headline 'ADIÓS CON BRONCA' (Goodbye with anger), setting a tone of turmoil as a narrator explains Real Madrid's immediate loss at home against Sevilla and the subsequent firing of Ramon. A split-screen shows a bald man and a man with glasses, likely commentators, discussing the situation. The bald man is then shown in a close-up, pointing at his face with a mischievous grin, as a quote appears: 'If an aeroplane crashed in Colombia, he would blame the coach.' The narrator clarifies this quote is attributed to Sacchi, highlighting the intense scrutiny and blame culture under the club’s leadership. The scene transitions to a press conference where the bald man, now identified by the narrator as the Brazilian Luxemburgo, Real Madrid's third manager of the season, stands beside others with sponsor logos like Siemens and Audi in the background, marking his arrival. A montage follows, showing Luxemburgo in a blue Brazil national team jacket, confirming his past role as Brazil's coach, before he is seen holding a Real Madrid jersey with 'GRAVESEN 16', as the narrator explains Gravesen was brought in to fill the defensive midfield spot and bring balance. A graphic lists early wins against teams like Atlético, Zaragoza, and Mallorca, supporting the narrator's mention of a winning streak. However, this quickly shifts to a newspaper clipping titled 'ADIÓS MADRID' and archival footage of a 2005 Copa del Rey match (Madrid 1-1 Valladolid) and a Champions League defeat to Juventus (2-1), as the narrator details their early exit from both competitions. The segment culminates with a dramatic goal celebration by Trezeguet in a Juventus jersey, accompanied by a loud crowd cheer and an excited commentator shouting "GOAL!", underscoring the team’s decline and the failure of Luxemburgo’s tenure, though the narrator briefly mentions a big 4-2 win in the Clasico at the very end.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dynamic montage of Real Madrid’s 2004-05 season struggles, beginning with a league table from April 10, 2005, displaying Real Madrid in second place with 63 points, trailing Barcelona’s 69, under the Spanish text 'Reválida a la presión blanca'. A narrator explains that despite Real Madrid's efforts "against Barcelona," the "Blaugrana still maintained a comfortable gap in the La Liga." This is followed by a tense face-to-face confrontation between two Real Madrid players in white jerseys, identified as Luis Figo, with the on-screen text 'FIGO X LUX' suggesting internal conflict. The narrator clarifies this moment "signaled the beginning of Figo's rift with the Brazilian manager, after being subbed in for the last eight minutes." A graphic then displays a series of match results against teams like Levante, Villarreal, and Real Racing Club, with times like '17’', '13’', and '0’', while a distressed Figo covers his face in frustration. The scene shifts to black-and-white footage of a Real Madrid player in a black 'SIEMENS mobile' jersey, as the narrator reveals "the Portuguese didn't start once in the last seven matches of the season, which opened the door to many rumors on his departure from the club." A subsequent league table shows Barcelona at 84 points and Real Madrid at 80, with the bold headline 'MISCA EL BARCA!', confirming the narrator's statement that "Madrid ended another season without a title, and in second place behind champions Barcelona." The segment transitions to a black screen with '05-06 Season' in white, accompanied by a shift in the background music to a more somber tone. The new season begins with a close-up of a dejected Figo in a black shirt, as the narrator explains "the new season started as the previous one has ended, as Figo's relationship was still complicated with the Brazilian manager." A newspaper clipping with the headline 'Problemas para facturar a Figo' highlights his contractual issues, further elaborated by the narrator stating that "after arriving with the team in preseason, Figo didn't participate in any match." The final frame features a coach in a green 'SIEMENS' training vest holding a football, accompanied by the quote 'If a footballer wants to leave, let him do so now,' which the narrator explicitly attributes to "Luxemburgo," solidifying the new managerial stance.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a newspaper-style graphic titled 'FIGO ITALIANO,' juxtaposing a smiling Luis Figo in a Real Madrid jersey against a photo of Samuel Eto’o holding an Inter Milan jersey, with a quote from Samuel stating 'La experiencia ha sido negativa.' A narrator explains that this "Galactico left Los Blancos, as Luis Figo signed with Inter Milan," adding that Figo "was quoted saying that he was disappointed with the man who led the club," and that "both Samuel and Solari joined him at the Italian club as well." The scene transitions to a newspaper headline 'El Newcastle, a por Owen,' featuring Michael Owen in a Newcastle United jersey, with the narrator stating that "after a somewhat good season, Owen went back to England." The focus then shifts to a split-screen comparing transfer fees: €20.0M for Baptista and €24.0M for Robinho, both in Real Madrid kits. The narrator clarifies that "Los Blancos didn't bring a superstar, rather they brought numerous players like the Brazilian duo of Baptista and the young Robinho." The narrative continues with Sergio Ramos, shown holding a Real Madrid jersey with 'SERGIO RAMOS 4' and a transfer fee of €27.0M, smiling broadly. The narrator identifies him as "the player who was labeled the first Spanish Galactico, Sergio Ramos at only 19 years of age," noting his release clause was "worth 27 million euros." The narrative continues with match footage of Real Madrid players in action, including a close-up of a player with long hair (likely Woodgate) looking intense. The narrator comments that "Madrid's start to the season was average to say the least, as Jonathan Woodgate finally came back after a whole season out because of injuries." This is followed by a wide shot of a goal being scored, which the narrator reveals was an "own goal" by Woodgate in his "first official match," adding that he "was sent off after two cautions as well." The segment concludes with a newspaper graphic titled 'DESPITE WOODGATE,' showing a league table with Real Madrid in second place, and a photo of Woodgate in a yellow jersey, highlighting the team’s resilience despite his controversial debut, as the narrator begins to say, "But despite that, Los Blancos..." before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a La Liga classification table, highlighting Real Madrid at the top with 15 points after match day 7, as the narrator explains Barcelona's poor start to the season. The scene transitions to a match between Real Madrid and Barcelona in November 2005, where Ronaldinho, in his iconic blue and red striped Barcelona jersey, scores a goal in the 77th minute, leading to a 3-0 victory. During the goal, a Spanish commentator excitedly shouts 'Ronaldinho!' multiple times, followed by 'Gol!' and 'Golazo!', as the narrator remarks that Ronaldinho 'demonstrated a lesson in football'. Ronaldinho celebrates by pointing skyward, while the Real Madrid goalkeeper looks dejected. The narrator notes that 'The Brazilian was given a big ovation by the fans', though Salgado viewed it as 'a way of punishing the Madrid players'. The narrative then shifts to the aftermath, showing a close-up of a distressed Real Madrid coach, Luxemburgo, in a blue tracksuit, as the narrator explains he 'survived for three more matches' before being 'sacked'. This is followed by newspaper headlines announcing his departure ('LUXE, A LA CALLE') and Sacchi’s exit, with the narrator adding that Juan Caro became caretaker manager and Sacchi left due to dissatisfaction. The segment concludes with a graphic of winter transfers, featuring Cassano for €5.5M and Cicinho for €4.0M in Real Madrid’s white jerseys, symbolizing the club’s attempt to rebuild, though the narrator states these transfers 'didn't change much at the club'.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a tense visual of Real Madrid’s Ronaldo looking downcast as a Zaragoza player gestures upward, setting a tone of conflict, while a male narrator, accompanied by a dramatic background score, details the "historic match" in early February where Madrid faced Real Zaragoza in the Copa del Rey semi-final. A graphic dated 'FEB 8, 2006' then appears, marking the date of this heavy defeat, as the narrator confirms Real Madrid "lost in a historic match by six goals to one." Visually, a dejected Casillas is shown alongside a composed Milito, with archival footage following of Casillas in a red jersey, visibly distressed, and a wide shot displaying the 'ZARAGOZA 6-1 MADRID' score. The narrator attributes "four goals by Diego Milito" to this loss, punctuated by a brief, excited Spanish commentary during a goal. The narrative shifts to a Champions League match against Arsenal, with players shaking hands pre-game, as the narrator explains Madrid faced a "tough test against Arsenal" in the round of 16. A close-up of Ronaldo’s intense gaze is accompanied by a quote overlay: 'Nunca he entendido a la afición del Bernabéu, que no es la misma que me demuestra cariño en la calle', with the narrator stating Ronaldo "criticized his own fans" and said the Bernabéu "never showed him any love." The crowd is shown waving white and purple flags, symbolizing fan support, before cutting to Henry scoring for Arsenal, with the score 'MADRID 0-1 ARSENAL' displayed, a goal the narrator credits to Henry. The segment concludes with a quote from Carlos Pinedo: 'Pensó más en él que en el equipo', criticizing Ronaldo’s self-centeredness, as footage of Ronaldo and teammates in white jerseys shows them walking off the pitch in defeat, with the narrator revealing that "Captain Raul criticized his Brazilian teammate" (Ronaldo) for "being selfish and thought more about himself than the team."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a filmstrip montage of dejected Real Madrid players in white 'SIEMENS' jerseys, walking off the pitch in the rain after a 2-1 loss to Mallorca, a defeat the narrator confirms extended their gap from leader Barcelona to 10 points. The visual then shifts to a La Liga table highlighting Real Madrid in third place with 48 points, further emphasizing their struggles. A black-and-white newspaper graphic appears, declaring 'EL FINAL DE LA ERA GALÁCTICA' and 'DIMISIÓN' over Florentino Pérez, whose resignation is confirmed by the narrator, along with his quote about spoiling the players. A close-up visual of a man identified as José Mourinho speaking into a microphone is shown with the quote 'From now on, I will play with players not names,' though the narrator attributes this philosophy shift to manager Caro. The video continues with a graphic of Mourinho shouting at a grid of teams (Valencia, Betis, Zaragoza, Barca, Sociedad, Getafe), visually representing a series of draws, which the narrator explains were six out of seven La Liga matches, killing their title hopes after crashing out of the Champions League. Archival footage shows a Real Madrid player scoring against Barcelona, followed by a dejected player after a 1-1 draw with Sociedad in April 2006. The narrator also mentions Ronaldo's 100th goal and subsequent injury. The segment concludes with Zinedine Zidane, bald and in a white Real Madrid jersey, at a press conference with the quote 'Au revoir' and the date April 25, 2006, as the narrator confirms Zidane's announcement to retire after the World Cup in the summer.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a filmstrip-style montage of Real Madrid players in white jerseys with 'SIEMENS' sponsorship, visibly dejected in the rain after a 2-1 loss to Mallorca on February 26, 2000, setting a tone of disappointment. This transitions to a close-up of the La Liga table, highlighting Real Madrid’s 48 points, third place, and the growing gap to Barcelona’s 58 points, emphasizing the team’s struggles. The scene shifts to a black-and-white newspaper graphic with the headline 'EL FINAL DE LA ERA GALÁCTICA' and a large 'DIMISIÓN' (Resignation) over a man in a suit and tie, identified as Florentino Pérez, who is quoted as saying 'He spoiled a los jugadores' (He spoiled the players). The narrative continues with a close-up of a man in a dark suit, later identified as José Mourinho, speaking into a microphone with the quote 'From now on, I will play with players not names,' suggesting a shift in philosophy. The video then cuts to a graphic of Mourinho shouting at a grid, listing teams like Valencia, Betis, Zaragoza, Barca, Sociedad, and Getafe, indicating a series of draws. This is followed by archival footage of a Real Madrid player in a white jersey scoring against Barcelona, then a dejected Real Madrid player after a 1-1 draw with Sociedad in April 2006. The final scene shows a bald man in a white Real Madrid jersey, identified as Zinedine Zidane, at a press conference with the quote 'Au revoir' and the date April 25, 2006, marking his departure. The overall atmosphere is one of decline, resignation, and transition, underscored by stark visuals and critical quotes.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white shot of Zinedine Zidane celebrating a goal, marked by on-screen text 'ZIDANE – 66’', as a narrator describes a player scoring in his last match at the Santiago Bernabéu, announcing "the end of an era" without good signs for the future. This transitions to a L'Équipe newspaper cover featuring Zidane waving, symbolizing his emotional farewell. The scene then shifts to black-and-white portraits, including a dejected Real Madrid player and Florentino Pérez in a suit, while the narrator explains that despite "titles having been absent" and "many managers and directors having been recycled," Florentino Pérez succeeded in his initial plan to turn Real Madrid into a "commercial and profitable machine." A Deloitte-branded graphic then displays Real Madrid leading 'TOTAL REVENUES €M' for 2004/05 and 2005/06, which the narrator confirms is based on "Deloitte's report on the richest clubs in the world," where Madrid claimed the top spot. A close-up of a smiling Florentino Pérez appears as a pie chart titled 'Madrid’s Revenue Mix 2005/2006' overlays the screen, detailing Broadcasting Revenues at 42%, Matchday at 30%, and Commercial at 28%. The narrator emphasizes that Pérez achieved this by "shifting and changing the revenue composition," with broadcasting contracts making up the "biggest share of revenue at the club" at 42%, even through his resignation in February 2006. A dramatic, slightly melancholic instrumental background music plays throughout the segment.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white shot of a Real Madrid player in a white jersey, identified as Zinedine Zidane, celebrating a goal against Villarreal in a 3-3 La Liga match from 2005/06, with the on-screen text 'ZIDANE – 66’' marking the moment. The scene transitions to a newspaper cover of L'Équipe, featuring a black-and-white image of Zidane waving to the crowd with the headline 'DE BEAUX ADIEUX' (Beautiful Goodbyes), symbolizing his farewell. The narrative then shifts to a series of black-and-white portraits: first, a bald man in a Real Madrid training jersey with 'SIEMENS' sponsorship, then a composite image of the same man playfully posing with two others, followed by a formal portrait of a man in a suit and tie—Florentino Pérez—centered between the two images. The video then cuts to a Deloitte-branded graphic displaying 'TOTAL REVENUES €M' for 2004/05, with Real Madrid leading at €275M, followed by Manchester United, AC Milan, Juventus, and Chelsea. This is updated to 2005/06, with Real Madrid at €292M, now followed by FC Barcelona, Juventus, Manchester United, and AC Milan. A close-up of Florentino Pérez appears, smiling, as a pie chart titled 'Madrid’s Revenue Mix 2005/2006' overlays the screen, showing Broadcasting Revenues at 42%, Matchday Revenues at 30%, and Commercial Revenues at 28%, sourced from Deloitte Football Money League. The sequence ends with the pie chart fully displayed, emphasizing the club’s financial structure under Pérez’s leadership.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a pie chart titled 'Madrid’s Revenue Mix 2005/2006', sourced from Deloitte Football Money League, displaying Commercial Revenues at 43%, Matchday Revenues at 26%, and Broadcasting Revenues at 31%, while a portrait of Florentino Pérez is visible. A male narrator explains that 'all of that shifted in 2006 as Madrid had its biggest share of revenues coming from commercial activities at 43%.' The scene transitions to a graphic titled 'The “Galácticos” Policy', featuring a world map and four Real Madrid players—Luis Figo, David Beckham, Zinedine Zidane, and Ronaldo—as the narrator attributes this shift 'mainly because of the Galácticos policy, which is a big factor in attracting global fans.' A colorful, abstract transition follows, during which the narrator reflects that 'when we determine the success and failure of this era, opinions may differ.' The video then shows a black-and-white shot of Florentino Pérez smiling beside another man, followed by a serious black-and-white portrait of Pérez against blurred newspaper text with 'DIMISIÓN' (Resignation) and 'FLORENTINO PRESIDENTE'. The narrator states that 'Pérez resigned and lost the players and the fans,' but 'he laid the blueprint for Los Blancos and maintained the financial health of the club for an extended period,' adding that 'he comes back in the following years with a new perspective, acknowledging his mistakes so that he succeeds again.' The scene abruptly cuts to live-action football footage of a goalkeeper in a blue kit diving to save a shot, with 'UniCredit' visible on advertising hoardings, accompanied by intense, fast-paced Spanish commentary and a 'GOL!' sound effect, followed by crowd cheering. This action fades to black, and a dramatic, slightly melancholic instrumental music track begins, playing over the final credit screen which lists the writer, director, narrator (Fahad Al-Thekair), sources, and music credits.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a pie chart titled 'Madrid’s Revenue Mix 2005/2006', sourced from Deloitte Football Money League, showing Commercial Revenues at 43% (yellow), Matchday Revenues at 26% (blue), and Broadcasting Revenues at 31% (red), with Florentino Pérez’s portrait on the right. The scene transitions to a graphic titled 'The “Galácticos” Policy', featuring a world map and four Real Madrid players—Luis Figo, David Beckham, Zinedine Zidane, and Ronaldo—holding a jersey with 'RONALDO 11' and 'FIGO 10' visible, symbolizing the club’s star-studded policy. segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment begins with a static, black-and-white credit screen featuring a grid background. The text lists the creator, Fahad Al-Thekair, as the writer, director, and narrator, followed by a detailed list of sources including Mundo Deportivo, Real Madrid’s official website, The Guardian, and others. The music credits are also displayed, listing artists such as Ghost Factory, Stray Theories, and Dawn of Midi. A vertical white bar runs down the right side of the screen, adding a structural element to the layout. After a few seconds, the screen fades to black, and the word 'balón' appears in a stylized, light blue font with a double-circle design on the 'o' and a small accent mark above the 'n'. The logo remains on screen for several seconds before fading out, marking the end of the segment. The overall atmosphere is minimalistic and professional, emphasizing the production’s credibility and branding.

entity_library.Bald man in grey top: A bald man, initially seen in a split-screen and then in a close-up with a mischievous grin, pointing at his face, visually representing the quote about blame. He is later identified by the narrator as Luxemburgo, Real Madrid's new Brazilian manager, and is seen at a press conference and holding Gravesen's jersey.

entity_library.Champions League trophy: A large, silver, ornate trophy with two handles, held aloft by Real Madrid players in celebration, symbolizing their 1998 victory, which the narrator states Real Madrid won twice in three years.

entity_library.Financial data overlay: On-screen text displaying 'TOTAL REVENUES €M' for 2004/05 and 2005/06, with club crests and revenue figures, providing context on Real Madrid’s financial dominance, which the narrator confirms was the top spot according to Deloitte's report.
entity_library.Florentino Pérez: A man in a suit and tie with glasses, shown in a formal black-and-white portrait, later smiling in a color close-up, representing the club’s president and financial architect. The narrator explicitly refers to him as 'Pérez' when discussing his resignation, his role in maintaining the club's financial health, and his eventual return with a new perspective, acknowledging his mistakes.

entity_library.Archival footage: Grainy, live-action footage of football matches, including the 2006 Copa del Rey semi-final (Zaragoza 6-1 Madrid) and the Champions League match (Madrid 0-1 Arsenal), showing goals and player reactions, providing context for the team’s struggles, with the narrator detailing the scores and key players.
entity_library.Santiago Bernabéu: An older man in a suit, smiling beside another man, identified by on-screen text as Santiago Bernabéu, symbolizing the club’s historical leadership. The narrator mentions him as an 'ex-president' who brought the best stars to Madrid, a legacy Florentino Pérez aimed to replicate.
